prompt: A real photograph from the James Webb Space Telescope NIRCam instrument. Deep field observation of an empty region of space. 16:9. The image is 95% pure black void. Almost nothing is here. In the far distance, barely visible, a hairline fracture runs vertically through the dark — a gravitational anomaly where spacetime has bent light around an invisible mass. The fracture is not glowing — it is defined only by the way background starlight bends and stretches around it, creating thin distorted arcs of pale warm light, the color of distant aged starlight (#A89060). The distortion is subtle, scientific, unsettling. A few dozen pinpoint stars scattered across the frame — sharp, tiny, some with 6-pointed JWST diffraction spikes. Two or three extremely faint smudges of distant galaxy clusters, barely distinguishable from noise, warm reddish tone from extreme redshift. The entire upper right corner fades into a massive dark molecular cloud — not a nebula, not colorful — just a region where there are NO stars, pure absence, slightly darker than the surrounding black. The bottom left has the faintest trace of warm infrared emission — dark rust (#0F0805), like heat signature from something ancient. This image is boring to a casual viewer. It is terrifying to an astrophysicist. The blacks are crushed. The highlights are almost nonexistent. ISO 50 equivalent. No noise. No grain. No artistic interpretation. No color grading. Raw telescope data. The feeling is: you are looking at something impossibly far away, impossibly old, and impossibly wrong.
